rulings.Case Title: STEVEN JAY ECHTMAN VS. KOSTOPOULOS LAW GROUP PC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Strike Or In The Alternative To Tax Costs; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF STEVEN ECHTMAN's Motion To Strike Or In The Alternative To Tax Costs Plaintiff Steven Jay Echtman's motion to strike or, in the alternative, tax costs is denied. Plaintiff's fee waiver, granted on March 2, 2016, does not excuse him from paying defendants' recoverable costs, to which they are entitled as a matter of right because they prevailed on summary judgment. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1032(b).) All four categories of costs claimed by defendants are explicitly authorized by Section 1033.5(a) as recoverable costs. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1033.5(a)(1), (3)(A), (11), and (14).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DAN DIAZ ET AL VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Dan Diaz To Attend Independent Medical Examination
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT ARMANDO BRITO's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Dan Diaz To Attend Independent Medical Examination. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion to compel plaintiff's attendance at a noticed medical examination is unopposed and granted. Plaintiff shall appear and permit examination completion on or before October 31, 2019 in Arizona or on such other date and time and different location to which counsel agree. If the plaintiff fails to appear for medical examination within said time frame there will be no further opportunity to do so and defendant may move for evidentiary sanctions based on violation of this Order. Monetary sanctions are awarded as against plaintiff personally in the amount of $1,110.00. Plaintiff shall also reimburse defendant in the sum of $1,800.00 for the costs incurred from his failure to appear at the previously scheduled medical examination. The court notes plaintiff made no attempt to advise his counsel or the examining physician of his last minute unavailability. Both the monetary sanction and reimbursement shall be paid within forty-five days of service of a copy of this Order with Notice of Entry. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DAJERVU TORREY VS. MV PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Its Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $5,800.75
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 12, 1) DEFENDANT MV TRANSPORTATION, INC.'s Motion To Compel Further Responses To Its Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $5,800.75 The report and recommendation of the judge pro tem is adopted. Defendant MV Transportation, Inc.'s motion to compel further response to its request for production of documents, set one is granted in part. Plaintiff shall provide verified responses (objection-free except as to privilege or work product) and documents within 14 days of service of this order as follows: RFD Nos. 6, 7, 8, 22, 30, 31, 35, 36, 56, 65, 67, 68, 69, 72, 73, 74, 75, 76, 83, 84, and 94. If privilege is claimed, plaintiff shall produce a privilege log identifying the name of the document, type of document, date created, preparer, recipient, general subject matter and legal reason for withholding. The motion is denied as to RPD 15, 24, 25, 79, 80, 85, 86, 87, 88, and 92 (statement that after diligent search, no documents exist is sufficient) and 18-19 (under right of privacy, discovery into plaintiff's entire medical history without limitation as to specific physical or mental condition(s) that has been placed in issue is not permitted under Britt v. Superior Court (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 864). "Mere speculation as to the possibility that some portion of the records might be relevant to some substantive issue does not suffice." (Davis v. Superior Court (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 1008, 1018.) The party seeking access to constitutionally protected information has the burden of proving the direct relevance of the information sought. (Id. at 1017.) Defendant's request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: NORTHERN CALIFORNIA COLLECTION SERVICE, INC. VS. MARGARITO PAZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Set Aside Default And Default Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT MARGARITO PAZ's Motion To Set Aside Default And Default Judgment Defendant Margarito Paz's motion to set aside default is granted. To obtain relief under Code of Civ. Proc., § 473.5, the moving party must show that "service of summons has not resulted in actual notice . . . in time to defend the action." Defendant's declaration sufficiently meets that burden, setting forth that he did not have actual notice because he no longer lived at the address at which plaintiff attempted substituted service, the McLaughlin residence, and because of his estranged relationship with his sister, who did not notify him that she had received the summons and complaint. (Paz Decl. 3-4, 6, 17 ["I had no idea that they had sued me."]; Second Paz Decl. 2, 4, 5, 15; see Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 547 ["'[A]ctual notice' in section 473.5 'means genuine knowledge of the party litigant.' [Citation.] 'Actual knowledge' has been strictly construed, with the aim of implementing the policy of liberally granting relief so that cases may be resolved on their merits."].) Defendant shall file an answer to the complaint within 30 days of entry of the court's order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: VIRGINIA ERNSTER ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the second cause of action for negligent entrustment is sustained without leave to amend. That claim is properly characterized as a direct claim against CCSF and plaintiff fails to highlight a statutory basis to support the claim in compliance with Gov't Code § 815. In C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 865-866, the California Supreme Court found that a school district may be vicariously liable under Gov't Code § 815.2 for the negligent supervision of its administrators despite the restrictions of the California Tort Claims Act. According to plaintiff, if a school district administrator can be liable for negligently hiring a teacher, then a City administrator can be liable for negligently entrusting the ambulance to Ms. Couch, its driver. The holding of C.A. is limited, however. The decision in that case rested on the "protective duty of ordinary care" that school districts owe their students. (See id. at 865, 877 ["Absent such a special relationship, there can be no individual liability to third parties for negligent hiring, retention or supervision of a fellow employee, and hence no vicarious liability under section 815.2...."].) In this case, there is no special relationship alleged that would support plaintiff's claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HERBAN INDUSTRIES CA LLC VS. EAZE TECHNOLOGIES, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT EAZE TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT: Defendant Eaze Technologies, Inc.'s demurrer to the complaint is overruled. Even if the court agrees that plaintiff fails to plead sufficient facts to support a UCL claim under the fraudulent prong, it pleads sufficient facts to support a claim under the unfair and unlawful prongs. With regard to a fraud prong claim, "[m]ost courts have concluded that Plaintiffs must allege their own reliance on the alleged misrepresentations, rather than the reliance of third parties." (Taxi Coop., Inc. v. Uber Techs., Inc., 114 F.Supp.3d 852, 866 (N.D. Cal. 2015).) Plaintiff cannot do so in this case. An unfair and unlawful prong claim does not employ the same reliance analysis, however. "When a plaintiff who claims to have suffered injury from a direct competitor's 'unfair' act or practice invokes section 17200, the word 'unfair' in that section means conduct that threatens an incipient violation of an antitrust law, or violates the policy or spirit of one of those laws because its effects are comparable to or the same as a violation of the law, or otherwise significantly threatens or harms competition." (Cel-Tech Communications, Inc. v. Los Angeles Cellular Telephone Co. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 163, 187 (emphasis added); see also Law Offices of Mathew Higbee v. Expungement Assistance Servs. (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 544, 547 [holding that "the lack of direct dealing between two business competitors is not necessarily fatal to UCL standing, provided the plaintiff competitor has suffered injury in fact and lost money or property as a result of the defendant competitor's unfair competition."]; see id. at 564 [rejecting as unpersuasive defendant's citation to cases pertaining to the requirement to allege reliance in order to show causation in cases based on fraud].) Plaintiff alleges that defendant's unfair and illegal conduct allowed it to obtain funds from the credit card companies and that conduct caused plaintiff financial harm. (Complaint, 64, 72-76, and 81.) The court rejects defendant's mootness argument. Whether plaintiff is continuing its practices is reasonably subject to dispute and judicial notice of defendant's screenshots-even if appropriate-is not dispositive. In addition, "many courts have rejected arguments against injunctive relief where defendants changed their practices only in response to being sued." (Aguilar v. Avis Rent A Car System, Inc. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 121, 133.) Lastly, the court rejects defendant's abstention argument. This case involves whether defendant's allegedly anti-competitive conduct has caused plaintiff harm. Resolution of the case will not embroil the court in the dual sovereignty thicket of cannabis regulation. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANNE EVANS VS. UNITY TECHNOLOGIES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT UNITY TECHNOLOGIES' Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings [part 1 of 2] Defendant's motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is granted. Plaintiff does not dispute that the parties entered into a valid arbitration agreement, but argues that Defendant waived its right to compel arbitration. Determination of waiver is a question of fact, not to be lightly inferred. (St. Agnes Medical Center v. PacifiCare of California (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1187, 1195.) The party who resists arbitration carries a heavy burden of proof and doubts regarding a waiver allegation should be resolved in favor of arbitration. (Id.) As Plaintiff acknowledges, courts consider the following factors when determining waiver: (1) whether the party's actions are inconsistent with the right to arbitrate, (2) whether the litigation machinery has been substantially invoked and the parties were well into preparation of a lawsuit before the party notified the opposing party of an intent to arbitrate, (3) whether a party either requested arbitration enforcement close to the trial date or delayed for a long period before seeking a stay, (4) whether a defendant seeking arbitration filed a counterclaim without asking for a stay of the proceedings, (5) whether important intervening steps (e.g., taking advantage of judicial discovery procedures that were not available in arbitration) had taken place, and (6) whether the delay affected, misled, or prejudiced the opposing party. (Id. at p. 1196.) Plaintiff argues that Defendant waived its right to arbitration by filing an answer that alleged facts to support its affirmative defenses. (Opp. at 4 n.1.) Defendant's answer, however, complies with the requirements of the Code (see Code Civ. Proc., § 431.30(b)(2)), and does not display any of the factors courts consider when assessing waiver. (See St. Agnes Medical Center v. PacifiCare of California, 31 Cal.4th at 1196.) By filing that answer, or allegedly disseminating a "mass email and Slack message" regarding Plaintiff's claims, Defendant did not engage in "procedural gamesmanship" remotely comparable to that addressed in Christensen v. Dewor Developments (1983) 33 Cal.3d 778, where the Court found that a party that "filed a lawsuit over admittedly arbitrable disputes, for the asserted purpose of discovering his opponent's legal theories, and . . . pursued that litigation through successive demurrers, abandoning it only a day before a scheduled hearing on the adequacy of his amended complaint," had engaged in a "deliberate end-run around the arbitral process" and thereby waived its right to arbitrate. (Id. at 780; see also id. at 783 [plaintiffs "admit that they intended all along to avail themselves of arbitration, but filed their superior court action only to obtain a set of verified pleadings from defendants which would reveal their opponents' legal strategies and theories"].) Indeed, if anyone has engaged in gamesmanship in this case, it is Plaintiff. If she wished to avoid having her dispute with Defendant aired in a public forum, she could have complied with her arbitration agreement by filing a claim in arbitration in the first place, rather than publicly filing a complaint with this Court. (See id. ["Among the principal reasons motivating persons to agree to arbitrate their differences is likely to be the avoidance of the courtroom with the attendant delays, costs, and publicity . . . ."].) Plaintiff's position-that after she publicly leveled serious accusations at Defendant, it could not respond to those allegations in kind without waiving its right to arbitration-is a textbook example of chutzpah. (See People v. Whigam (1984) 158 Cal.App.3d 1161, 1167 n.10 [defining chutzpah as "brazenness" or "gall"].) [See part 2 of 2 for continued tentative ruling] =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANNE EVANS VS. UNITY TECHNOLOGIES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Unity Technologies' Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings [Part 2 Of 2 For Long Tentative]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 10, 2019, Line 9, Defendant Unity Technologies' Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings [Part 2 Of 2 For Long Tentative] (Tentative ruling continued from previous entry.) The Court finds Defendant did not waive its right to arbitration and grants Defendant's motion to compel arbitration. The Court also grants Defendant's motion to stay proceedings pending arbitration. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 1281.4.)
